Commissiorz. 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Auistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Darrell W. Larsen 
Sutter County Counsel 
463 Second Street 
Yuba City, CA 
9S991 
Dear Mr. Larsen: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
March 8, 1983 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322·6444 
Re: 
Your Memorandum of 2/24/83; 
Our File No. A-83-0S0 
You have sent to me your memorandum regarding the summary of 
our telephone conversation regarding possible conflicts of 
interest with respect to a cable underground ordinance being 
considered recently by your Board of Supervisors. 
You tele-
phoned me for advice consultation on the afternoon of 
February 22, 1983. 
You advised me that the Board would be 
considering an ordinance that nignt dealing with a proposal by 
the Cable TV franchise which would require a developer, who was 
subdividing property and putting in improvements, to reimbu:se 
the Cable TV company for the cost of putting its caDle under-
ground. 
The usual practice is to put the TV cable underground 
along with other utilities which currently are required to be 
put underground. 
The cable company's pro rata snare of the 
undergrounding cost is currently oorne oy them and they desire 
to shift tne burden to tne developer. 
Three of your Board members have some ties with develop-
ment. 
One developed some properties in the past, but has not 
done so in the last five years, he has no current plans to 
develop. 
A second one is an active developer who can be 
expected to continue developing in tne future. 
The third one 
has recently sold some property to a developer who is planning 
to develop the land. 
These boara members aSKed whether tney are disqualified from 
voting on the ordinance because of their development interests. 
If it 1S reasonably foreseeable tnat the ordinance will have a 
material financial effect on any board memoer's investments or 
sources of income, disqualification is required under Government 
Code Section 87100. 


Darrell W. Larsen 
Ma r c h 8, 19 8 3 
Page Two 
As to the first member, who has not been involved in any 
development in the past five years, has no current development 
plans and may re-enter the field only if the economy improves 
sufficiently, I advised that it is not foreseeable that the 
decision would materially affect his financial interest. 
The 
first paragraph of your memorandum is essentially correct as to 
this member of the Board. 
Your second paragraph correctly indicates that the second 
member may have a conflict Decause it is reasonably foreseeable 
that his current and future development interest may be affected 
by the decision. 
You properly explained that 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18702 sets forth the criteria for determining whether a 
decision will have a material financial effect on a business 
entity. 
You also were correct in noting that tne purchaser/ 
developer is a source of income to the last memoer. 
Since the 
purchaser/developer is a business entity, the test of 
materiality in Section 18702 should be applied to him. 
With tnese clarifications in mind, I confirm your memorandum 
summarizing our discussion. 
If you have any questons regarding 
the foregoing, please contact me at 916/322-5901. 
REL: i<m 
Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh

HARRELL w. LARSEN 
County Counsel 
OFFICE OF THE COUNTY COUNSEL 
COUNTY OF StrrTER 
463 SECOND STREET 
YUBA CITY, CAUFORNIA 95991 
Robert Lydick, AtiDrney 
Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
TIDO K Street 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Dear Mr. Lydick: 
PHONE 671 -
4830 
February 24, 1983 
DWAINE F. SHIELDS 
Deputy County Counsel 
Following our telephone conversation of February 22, 1983, I 
di~ated 
a brief memo covering the points we discussed. 
Would you please 
review the attached memorandum and let me know if what is set out 
there accurately summarizes our discussion. 
Again, thank you for your assistance. 
DARRELL W. LARSEN 
SUTTER COUNTY COUNSEL 
DWL: js 
Enclosure 


MEMORANDUM RE CONFLICT OF INTEREST 
The following is a summary of my discussion with Attorney Bob Lydick 
of the Fair Political Practices Legal Division (phone 322-5901.) 
There were two issues involved in the analysis of the potential 
conflict of interest of "developers" arising out of the proposed 
cable TV ordinance. 
In order for a conflict to exist the decision 
involved must result in both a material financial effect on the 
supervisor involved as well as be foreseeable. 
With regard to the 
foreseeability under current economic conditions, Mr. Lydick pointed 
out that if the decision 
by the developer as to whether or not to 
do projects turned on the general state of the economy, the level 
of his development activity was not foreseeable. 
That is, notwith-
standing the encouraging predictions heard from certain governmental 
and private sources, the future state of this country's economy is 
sufficiently uncertain that decisions to develop which are based 
on that economy are also unforeseeable. 
Further, if it were not 
foreseeable that any work would be done by the developer within the 
next five years because, for instance, of other commitments, the 
prospects for development were again too uncertain to be foreseeable. 
We then discussed what constituted material financial effects both 
in the case of a business entity and in the case of a source of 
income. 
Mr. Lydick interpreted California Administrative Code §18702 
to the effect that if the impact on a developer's gross revenue as 
a consequence of having to contribute to the cost of trenching 
exceeded $100,000, the effect was clearly material. 
If the impact 
was less than $1,000 it was cleai¥ not material. 
If the effect was 
some place between $1,000 and $100,000, the 1% test of §18702(b} (1) 
(A) (2) would have to be applied. 
Between $1,000 and $100,000 the 
impact would have to be greater than 1% of the gross revenue of the 
business entity involved in order for it to be material. 
Similar 
tests with regard to net income would have to be applied with regard 
to the business entity as well as any source of income.

2/23/83

sections.conclusion: For the first member (no development activity in five years, no current plans): disqualification is not required as it is not foreseeable the decision would materially affect his financial interest. For the second member (active developer): disqualification is required as it is reasonably foreseeable his current and future development interests may be affected. For the third member (recently sold property to a developer): the materiality test under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 must be applied to determine if the impact on the purchaser/developer as a source of income is material.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether three county supervisors with varying degrees of development involvement must be disqualified from voting on an ordinance affecting cable TV undergrounding costs borne by developers.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the first supervisor need not recuse himself, the second supervisor must recuse himself due to foreseeable material effects on his development interests, and for the third supervisor, the materiality analysis under Section 18702 must be applied based on whether the ordinance's impact exceeds applicable thresholds ($100,000 clearly material, under $1,000 clearly not material, $1,000-$100,000 requires 1% gross revenue test).
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes multiple components: initial FPPC response letter from Robert Leidigh (3/8/83), incoming request letter from County Counsel (2/24/83), memorandum summarizing phone discussion, and partial telephone advice form. Primary FPPC response clearly structured with analysis of three scenarios. Some handwritten annotations on telephone form are illegible.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter analyzing conflict of interest issues for three county supervisors voting on a cable TV undergrounding ordinance. The letter applies Government Code Section 87100 and Administrative Code Section 18702 to determine which supervisors must be disqualified based on foreseeability and materiality of financial effects.
